# parseurl

[![NPM Version][npm-version-image]][npm-url]
[![NPM Downloads][npm-downloads-image]][npm-url]
[![Node.js Version][node-version-image]][node-version-url]
[![Build Status][travis-image]][travis-url]
[![Test Coverage][coveralls-image]][coveralls-url]

Parse a URL with memoization.

## Installation

```sh
$ npm install parseurl
```

## API

```js
var parseurl = require('parseurl')
```

### parseurl(req)

Parse the URL of the given request object (req) and return the result.
The result is the same as `url.parse` in Node.js core.
Memoizes the parsed object on the request object as a performance optimization.

```js
var url = parseurl(req)
// => { protocol: null,
//      slashes: null,
//      auth: null,
//      host: null,
//      port: null,
//      hostname: null,
//      hash: null,
//      search: '?query=string',
//      query: 'query=string',
//      pathname: '/foo',
//      path: '/foo?query=string',
//      href: '/foo?query=string' }
```

### parseurl.original(req)

Parse the original URL of the given request object and return the result.
This works by trying to parse `req.originalUrl` if it is a string, otherwise
parses `req.url`. The result is the same as `url.parse` in Node.js core.
Memoizes the parsed object on the request object as a performance optimization.

```js
var url = parseurl.original(req)
// => { protocol: null,
//      slashes: null,
//      auth: null,
//      host: null,
//      port: null,
//      hostname: null,
//      hash: null,
//      search: '?query=string',
//      query: 'query=string',
//      pathname: '/foo',
//      path: '/foo?query=string',
//      href: '/foo?query=string' }
```

## Performance Benefits

This package provides a performance optimization for parsing the URL in
incoming request objects. This is particularly useful in Express.js routing,
where every request needs to have its URL parsed to determine which route
handler should be invoked.

The optimization comes from memoizing the parsed URL on the request object,
so any subsequent calls to parse the URL will use the stored object instead
of parsing the URL again. This is especially beneficial in Express.js
applications with complex middleware chains where the URL might be parsed
multiple times during a single request lifecycle.

### Example with Express.js

In Express.js applications, this package is used internally to efficiently
route requests to the appropriate handlers. For example, in an application
with routes for both '/' and '/evening', parseurl helps Express quickly
determine which route handler should process each incoming request:

```js
const express = require('express')
const app = express()

// parseurl is used internally by Express to efficiently parse the URL
// and route this request to the appropriate handler
app.get('/', (req, res) => {
  res.status(200).type('text/plain').send('Hello, World!\n')
})

app.get('/evening', (req, res) => {
  res.status(200).type('text/plain').send('Good evening\n')
})

app.listen(3000, '127.0.0.1', () => {
  console.log('Server running at http://127.0.0.1:3000/')
})
```

## Benchmarks

```
$ npm run-script bench

> parseurl@1.3.3 bench
> node benchmark/index.js

> Parsing URL "http://localhost:8888/foo/bar?q=1"

  node_modules/url parse(req.url):
   1,836,482 ops/sec ±0.71% (187 runs sampled)

  parseurl(req):
   4,309,431 ops/sec ±0.42% (189 runs sampled)

  parseurl.original(req):
   4,409,882 ops/sec ±0.83% (189 runs sampled)
```
